chapter_summary: Chapter 1 argues that current reading tests are invalid because they attempt to measure general skills when reading comprehension is actually a function of specific background knowledge and vocabulary. The author claims that the stagnation of reading scores among seventeen-year-olds, despite improvements in early reading mechanics, proves that high-stakes testing without a knowledge-based curriculum is a failed educational strategy.

claims.text: The only way to make standardized tests fair and productive is to base them on well-defined, knowledge-based curriculums.
claims.source_passage: There’s just one way to do that—to base them on well-defined, knowledge-based curriculums. There is no other way of making tests fair and productive.

claims.text: The average reading score of graduating seventeen-year-olds is a valid single measure of the academic quality of a school system.
claims.source_passage: If one had to choose a single measure of the academic quality of a school system, the average reading score of its graduating seventeen-year-olds would serve.

claims.text: Verbal scores at age seventeen predict college and career readiness as well as later economic success.
claims.source_passage: Verbal scores at that age predict students’ college and career readiness and their later economic success.

claims.text: A reading test is effectively a test of general knowledge and vocabulary.
claims.source_passage: A reading test is a test of general knowledge and vocabulary; it gauges a student’s ability to operate effectively within the public sphere.

claims.text: Any educational policy that fails to improve reading test scores is a failed policy because valid reading tests measure a student's initiation into the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: Since a valid reading test probes a student’s degree of initiation into the public sphere—a fundamental aim of education—any policy that lowers or neglects to improve test scores in reading is a failed educational policy.

claims.text: American reading scores for seventeen-year-olds declined significantly between 1988 and 2012.
claims.source_passage: For, in 2012, a decade after the test-intensive No Child Left Behind Act (NCLB) went into effect, the reading scores of seventeen-year-olds came in significantly lower than they had been in 1988 before NCLB was enacted.

claims.text: The cohort that attained the highest recorded age-13 NAEP reading scores went on to show lower scores at age 17.
claims.source_passage: This 2012 result for seventeen-year-olds seems particularly anomalous, because that same cohort four years earlier, at age thirteen, had attained the topmost score that NAEP had ever recorded—higher than any prior cohort except 1992, with which it was tied.

claims.text: High-stakes testing regimens under NCLB deeply affected the content taught by schools and teachers in early grades.
claims.source_passage: Those testing regimens will have deeply affected what schools and teachers taught them in their early grades.

claims.text: Intensive test preparation in early grades does not help long-term mature reading ability.
claims.source_passage: Yet all that intensive test prep did not, in the end, help their mature reading.

claims.text: NCLB-era testing regimens helped improve the mechanics of early reading, such as decoding fluency and accuracy.
claims.source_passage: That’s a troubling thought to me, since the testing regimens have clearly helped improve the mechanics of early reading (that is, the ability to sound out fast and effortlessly)—an important gain.

claims.text: The narrowing of reading achievement gaps in early grades is due to improved fluency scores, not long-term literacy gains.
claims.source_passage: Because of that the reading gaps between ethnic and racial groups have narrowed in those years, when fluency is an important part of the score. But, alas, those equity gaps begin widening again between ages thirteen and seventeen, when knowledge and vocabulary are decisive.

claims.text: Vocabulary size is determined by the cumulative knowledge acquired from birth to age seventeen, not by high school instruction alone.
claims.source_passage: The vocabulary size of seventeen-year-olds is not determined at age seventeen, or sixteen, or fifteen. It’s a plant of slow growth that is determined by the knowledge that has been gradually acquired from a child’s overall experience from birth to age seventeen.

claims.text: Early schooling is a critical factor in vocabulary growth, particularly for disadvantaged children.
claims.source_passage: Early schooling can play a major role in vocabulary growth, especially for disadvantaged students.

claims.text: The No Child Left Behind Act (NCLB) improved the mechanics of early reading, specifically the ability to sound out words effortlessly.
claims.source_passage: Younger students can now decode texts with more fluency and accuracy than they did before NCLB.

claims.text: Reading gaps between ethnic and racial groups narrow in early years when fluency is the primary component of test scores.
claims.source_passage: Because of that the reading gaps between ethnic and racial groups have narrowed in those years, when fluency is an important part of the score.

claims.text: Reading equity gaps widen significantly between ages thirteen and seventeen when vocabulary and knowledge become the decisive factors in comprehension.
claims.source_passage: But, alas, those equity gaps begin widening again between ages thirteen and seventeen, when knowledge and vocabulary are decisive.

claims.text: High schools are not responsible for the decline in reading performance or the widening of the gap in older students.
claims.source_passage: One might first be inclined to blame the high schools for undoing what the grade schools had achieved in reading. But as a specialist in reading comprehension I know that such an accusation is incorrect.

claims.text: A seventeen-year-old's vocabulary size is a cumulative result of knowledge acquired gradually from birth.
claims.source_passage: It’s a plant of slow growth that is determined by the knowledge that has been gradually acquired from a child’s overall experience from birth to age seventeen.

claims.text: Current early schooling practices have inadvertently depressed the vocabulary growth of students by age seventeen.
claims.source_passage: While current schooling in early grades has improved the sounding-out aspects of reading, we can infer from the NAEP results that it has depressed the vocabulary sizes of seventeen-year-olds.

claims.text: Schools are spending excessive time on test preparation and insufficient time on the wide knowledge acquisition required for vocabulary growth.
claims.source_passage: My conclusion, which sets the stage for this chapter, is that too much time is now being spent on test preparation in early grades, and too little time is being spent on gaining the wide knowledge required for a broad vocabulary.

claims.text: The decline in American public schooling quality began in the 1960s and 1970s.
claims.source_passage: That report showed that American public schooling had declined in quality during the 1960s and 1970s.

claims.text: High-stakes testing has caused schools to abandon the teaching of history and the arts in favor of formal 'reading' instruction.
claims.source_passage: a number of schools told me, after high-stakes reading tests came into effect under NCLB, that they could no longer teach history and the arts. They now were being made to teach 'reading' instead, with a strong emphasis on test preparation.

claims.text: Educational administrators incorrectly believe that drills in formal skills like 'making inferences' are more effective for raising scores than learning content like history or science.
claims.source_passage: Their higher-ups were under the impression that intense classes devoted to 'making inferences' and 'finding the main idea' would improve reading scores more effectively than learning about ancient Egypt or the solar system

claims.text: Misguided efforts to raise test scores through skills-based instruction are likely the cause of low scores among seventeen-year-olds.
claims.source_passage: By an iron law of unintended consequences, the low scores of seventeen-year olds were probably caused by misguided, time-consuming efforts to raise scores.

claims.text: Common Core State Standards tests will continue to depress reading competence unless schooling prioritizes knowledge acquisition in early grades.
claims.source_passage: This chapter will predict that the high-stakes reading tests that accompany the new Common Core State Standards are going to have a similar nugatory or depressing effect on the reading competence of our seventeen-year-olds of the future

claims.text: Excessive and poorly designed testing drains creative approaches from classrooms and takes time away from actual learning.
claims.source_passage: Done poorly, in excess, or without clear purpose, [tests] take valuable time away from teaching and learning, draining creative approaches from our classrooms.

claims.text: Formal comprehension skill drills, such as 'summarizing' or 'questioning the author,' provide only an initial benefit that quickly plateaus.
claims.source_passage: And indeed students do show an initial positive effect from practicing finding the main idea. But their progress quickly reaches a limit and then halts.

claims.text: Drills in formal comprehension skills have failed to raise mature reading scores.
claims.source_passage: Drills in formal comprehension skills have not raised mature reading scores; rather, they have taken up a lot of class time that could have been devoted to knowledge building.

claims.text: Schooling has been made boring and ineffective by test-prep exercises in formal skills like summarizing and questioning the author.
claims.source_passage: In language arts, for instance, schooling has been bent out of shape and made boring and ineffective by test-prep exercises in skills like “summarizing” and “questioning the author.”

claims.text: Students show an initial positive effect from practicing main-idea finding, but their progress quickly reaches a limit and halts.
claims.source_passage: And indeed students do show an initial positive effect from practicing finding the main idea. But their progress quickly reaches a limit and then halts.

claims.text: The emphasis on formal technique over subject-matter knowledge in early grades produces 17-year-olds who lack the vocabulary to understand mature public discourse.
claims.source_passage: This emphasis on technique at the expense of building subject-matter knowledge in early grades produces students who at age seventeen lack the knowledge and vocabulary to understand the mature language of newspapers, textbooks, and political speeches.

claims.text: Reading comprehension in any given case depends more on relevant knowledge than on formal strategies.
claims.source_passage: We know that the skill of reading comprehension in any given case depends more on relevant knowledge than on formal strategies.

claims.text: Reading strategies, once briefly learned, take care of themselves.
claims.source_passage: Once briefly learned, the strategies will take care of themselves.

claims.text: After decoding is mastered, relevant knowledge is the skill set that most reliably determines reading comprehension.
claims.source_passage: The basic principle for schools to keep in mind is that, once decoding has been mastered, the “skill set” that most reliably determines reading comprehension is relevant knowledge.

claims.text: The unpredictability of topics on current reading tests forces schools to focus on strategies and meaning-guessing rather than systematic knowledge acquisition.
claims.source_passage: The unpredictability of the test topics forces the schools to stress the externalities of test taking and of meaning guessing.

claims.text: Well-established reading tests like the Gates-MacGinitie and NAEP are technically reliable and valid measures of average reading ability.
claims.source_passage: Despite their indifference to the school curriculum, the better reading tests are technically reliable and valid. The Gates-MacGinitie reading test, the Iowa Test of Basic Skills, and the National Assessment of Educational Progress are good measures of the average reading abilities of large groups of students.

claims.text: Current standardized reading tests are consequentially invalid because they lead schools to engage in self-defeating educational practices.
claims.source_passage: to engage in self-defeating practices. They are consequentially invalid.

claims.text: The idea that there is a generalized, transferable 'main-idea-finding skill' is a myth.
claims.source_passage: By focusing on main-idea finding, the standards promote the myth that there is a generalized main-idea-finding skill, which if practiced and developed will enhance reading ability.

claims.text: Reading test questions about main ideas and inference-making are actually probing knowledge and vocabulary, not general strategies.
claims.source_passage: The test questions about main ideas and inference making imply the misleading message that they are probing all-purpose strategies and skills of predicting, summarizing, and “inferencing.” But they are doing no such thing. The tests are probing knowledge and vocabulary.

claims.text: The defect in modern testing lies primarily in the scientific shortcomings of content-free state standards rather than the tests themselves.
claims.source_passage: That defect lies less in the tests themselves than in the scientific shortcomings of the empty state standards on which they are based.

claims.text: Educational standards have misled schools regarding the actual nature of reading skill.
claims.source_passage: The standards have misled the schools regarding the nature of reading skill.

claims.text: Current student performance on tests disproves the existence of a transferable main-idea skill, as students heavily drilled in this skill are not seeing improved outcomes.
claims.source_passage: But if that were so for mature reading ability, the current generation of students would be performing better on the tests, for they have all been well schooled in main-idea finding.

claims.text: Standardized test questions regarding main ideas and inference do not actually probe all-purpose strategies like predicting or summarizing.
claims.source_passage: The test questions about main ideas and inference making imply the misleading message that they are probing all-purpose strategies and skills of predicting, summarizing, and “inferencing.” But they are doing no such thing.

claims.text: Standardized reading tests are actually probes of a student's knowledge and vocabulary.
claims.source_passage: The tests are probing knowledge and vocabulary.

claims.text: The No Child Left Behind Act's emphasis on decoding has been productive for improving the mechanics of early reading.
claims.source_passage: To the credit of the No Child Left Behind Act, the new focus on decoding has been highly productive for the mechanics of early reading.

claims.text: After a student masters decoding and attains fluency, relevant knowledge becomes the primary component of reading skill.
claims.source_passage: But once decoding has been mastered and fluency attained, relevant knowledge becomes the chief component of reading skill.

claims.text: Test makers imply a lie by suggesting their questions probe formal skills when they actually reward relevant vocabulary and knowledge.
claims.source_passage: No doubt unintentionally, and with inadequate knowledge of psycholinguistics, the test makers are implying a lie. By the form of their questions they suggest that they are probing formal skills. But, no matter how well trained students become in main-idea finding, the student with the smaller relevant vocabulary and knowledge is the one who will fare worse on the test.

claims.text: The 'opt-out' protest movement indicates that parents and teachers have correctly identified that the current testing system is failing.
claims.source_passage: That parents and teachers alike are demonstrating against the new tests, and opting out of them, is a pretty good indication that they have correctly concluded that something has gone wrong.

claims.text: Current test preparation narrows elementary schooling by displacing subjects like social studies, science, and the arts.
claims.source_passage: Teachers have complained that test preparation has narrowed elementary schooling—pushing out social studies, science, and the arts.

claims.text: Constant testing has placed unproductive stress on children and teenagers, leading to health and psychological issues.
claims.source_passage: Parents have complained of the neglect of history and the arts, adding that constant testing has placed unfruitful stress upon their children.

claims.text: State math standards are superior to reading standards because math standards are content-specific and build on each other progressively.
claims.source_passage: Current math standards are much better guides for teachers and test makers than are current standards in reading.

claims.text: American standards makers have avoided specifying content in reading due to fear of political controversy.
claims.source_passage: The makers of standards have decided that while it is politically feasible to create a definite content guide in math, fierce controversy would follow if they created a definite content guide in reading.

claims.text: Reading standards in states like Texas and Virginia are repetitive and 'content empty,' failing to provide a clear progression of topics across grades.
claims.source_passage: My own state of Virginia is more forthright about the inherent repetitiousness and content emptiness of its reading standard

claims.text: State educational standards focus on the skill of finding the main idea rather than specifying topics or texts.
claims.source_passage: instead of specifying either topics or texts, the standards makers focus on the skill of finding the main idea.

claims.text: The reading standards in Virginia are repetitious and lack substantive content across grade levels.
claims.source_passage: My own state of Virginia is more forthright about the inherent repetitiousness and content emptiness of its reading standards

claims.text: The Common Core State Standards in English Language Arts lack real progression in content across grade levels.
claims.source_passage: Nor are the new Common Core State Standards in English language arts exempt from this same lack of real progression or content

claims.text: A student's ability to identify a main idea on a test is a direct result of their understanding of the specific passage, not a transferable skill.
claims.source_passage: If readers understand a passage, they will reliably answer the test question about the main idea. If they don’t understand the passage, they won’t.

claims.text: Complex writing often contains multiple central ideas rather than a single 'main idea' as suggested by tests.
claims.source_passage: Moreover, in good, complex writing there isn’t usually a single main idea.

claims.text: Empty skills-based standards were created primarily for political expediency.
claims.source_passage: These empty standards were created out of political expediency. The makers of standards and tests have built up an artificial construct, politically painless for the makers of standards and of tests

claims.text: Reading comprehension is not an all-purpose skill like decoding.
claims.source_passage: Test makers have dutifully followed the standards makers, presenting reading comprehension as an all-purpose skill like decoding. Since it isn’t, the current standards and tests have created a fictional alternative universe in our classrooms.

claims.text: The ability to comprehend a text is dependent on having relevant knowledge and vocabulary.
claims.source_passage: In the real world, an ability to comprehend a piece of writing depends on one’s having the knowledge and vocabulary relevant to that passage.

claims.text: A reading test is an unfair measure of schooling if the school has not taught the specific knowledge required to understand the test passages.
claims.source_passage: If the school does not teach students the knowledge and vocabulary they need to understand the passages on the test, then the test is unfair as a measure of what the school has successfully taught.

claims.text: Current standardized reading tests primarily reflect knowledge students acquired outside of school.
claims.source_passage: The test simply reflects the knowledge and vocabulary that students have picked up from all sources.

claims.text: Test makers define reading as a set of 'strategies' to give the appearance of fairness in the absence of a shared curriculum.
claims.source_passage: a method has had to be devised that seems to make these inherently unfair tests fair. That method has been to define reading ability as a set of strategies, and then to create test items that appear to probe those strategies.

claims.text: The phrasing of test questions is misleadingly designed to suggest they test strategy expertise rather than specific content knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Their form indicates misleadingly that strategy expertise rather than specific knowledge is being probed.

claims.text: Texas standardized test items misleadingly indicate that strategy expertise rather than specific knowledge is being probed.
claims.source_passage: Their form indicates misleadingly that strategy expertise rather than specific knowledge is being probed.

claims.text: Reading comprehension test items are easy for students who understand the passage but impossible for those who do not, regardless of strategy training.
claims.source_passage: Answering such items is easy for students who understand the passages, but not for those who don’t—no matter how many drills in comprehension strategies the students had before the test.

claims.text: Relevant domain-specific background knowledge enables a student to understand a passage and answer questions fast without conscious strategizing.
claims.source_passage: A child who has the relevant domain-specific background knowledge will understand the passage and get the right answer fast, without conscious strategizing.

claims.text: A student lacking background knowledge must rely on conscious strategies and glosses, which prevents them from finishing the test and leads to errors.
claims.source_passage: A child who does not have enough relevant knowledge will have to use special glosses in the test and consciously apply strategies; that child won’t finish the test and will get many answers wrong.

claims.text: The educational system has devised a fictitious alternative world where metaskills are presented as being as important as knowledge and vocabulary to mask test unfairness.
claims.source_passage: In sum, to mask the inherent unfairness of these tests, a fictitious alternative world has been devised in which metaskills look as important as knowledge and vocabulary.

claims.text: Content-evading state standards define the reading curriculum as a set of all-purpose techniques like inference making and predicting.
claims.source_passage: The substance of the reading curriculum had been described in the content-evading state standards as consisting of all-purpose techniques like inference making, predicting, and main-idea finding.

claims.text: Standardized reading tests appear fair because they are 'standards-based,' even though they are inherently unfair to disadvantaged students.
claims.source_passage: Despite their unfairness (especially to disadvantaged students), they have thus been made to appear to be fair.

claims.text: Instruction in reading strategies offers a small initial score boost but reaches a plateau quickly and does not require ongoing practice.
claims.source_passage: Lessons in reading strategies offer an initial score boost for test taking, but are quickly learned; they plateau fast, and they don’t have to be practiced.

claims.text: The optimal amount of time to spend on reading comprehension strategy instruction is approximately two weeks.
claims.source_passage: Two weeks on comprehension strategies is optimal. There is no practical utility after that.

claims.text: Forcing students to become self-conscious about applying reading strategies distracts them and degrades their actual reading performance.
claims.source_passage: Worse, making young students become highly self-conscious about applying strategies distracts their attention and degrades their performance.

claims.text: Reading instruction and testing should be restructured to resemble math, with specific content standards and tests based on that content.
claims.source_passage: The structure will need to become more like that of math, with specific content in the standards and the curriculum, and with tests based on that content.

claims.text: General skills such as close reading, complexity managing, and main-idea finding do not actually exist as transferable skills.
claims.source_passage: high-stakes reading tests that, as before, pretend to test the general skills of close reading, complexity managing, and main-idea finding—general skills that do not exist.

claims.text: The most important words in the Common Core standards are those stating that standards can only be implemented through a curriculum structured to develop rich content knowledge.
claims.source_passage: They contain the welcome admonition that the standards can only be properly implemented through a “curriculum intentionally and coherently structured to develop rich content knowledge within and across grades.” Those precious words are to my mind the most important ones in the new standards.

claims.text: Common Core reading tests are unlikely to be more educationally productive than previous tests because they still lack grade-by-grade content guidance.
claims.source_passage: In the absence of specific grade-by-grade content guidance, the makers of the Common Core tests are placed in the same position as the makers of the previous tests... it will be hard for them to be any more educationally productive than the tests they are displacing.

claims.text: New reading tests based on state standards will not be any more educationally productive than the tests they are displacing.
claims.source_passage: So, despite the expense and computerized novelty of the new reading tests, it will be hard for them to be any more educationally productive than the tests they are displacing.

claims.text: The Common Core standards for main-idea finding and close reading are the most content-free, strategy-like elements of the framework.
claims.source_passage: Those are the Common Core standards that are the most content-free, strategy-like elements.

claims.text: Prior relevant knowledge of words and topics is the only way to successfully answer test questions that probe comprehension and inference.
claims.source_passage: the secret to answering such questions will not be hours of practice of 'inferencing skills' and 'close-reading skills,' but can only be answered through the student’s prior relevant knowledge of the words and the topics.

claims.text: Logical acumen cannot decide the meaning of a word when the context allows for multiple correct interpretations.
claims.source_passage: Anyway, logical acumen will not decide that particular issue.

claims.text: Current standardized reading tests are based on a wrong theory of inference and reading comprehension.
claims.source_passage: It’s based on a wrong theory of inference and reading comprehension.

claims.text: Inferences in reading tend to be of the part-whole situation model rather than the deductive-syllogistic model.
claims.source_passage: inferences tend to be of the part-whole situation model, not the deductive-syllogistic model.

claims.text: Knowledge-based schooling is the most promising way to narrow the reading gap between demographic groups.
claims.source_passage: It is by far the most promising way to... narrow the reading gap between demographic groups.

claims.text: Classroom exercises focused on general reading skills are knowledge-displacing, soul-deadening, and useless after ten lessons.
claims.source_passage: to replace exercises that are knowledge-displacing, soul-deadening, and essentially useless after ten lessons.

claims.text: Substantive knowledge gains constitute the most effective long-range strategy for reading comprehension.
claims.source_passage: When a student gains a real advance in substantive knowledge, it’s the best long-range comprehension strategy.

claims.text: Accountability in schools requires standards that are specific guides to curricular content.
claims.source_passage: Only standards that are guides to curricular content can be foundations for fair and productive tests.

claims.text: The meaning of phrases like 'remaining elements' in scientific contexts requires specific background knowledge that fifth graders typically lack.
claims.source_passage: Its meaning requires specific background knowledge of a highly sophisticated kind (probably already provided earlier in the source), which most fifth graders will normally lack.

claims.text: Time currently spent on skill-based reading exercises should instead be spent on subject matters that build knowledge and vocabulary to induce reading competence.
claims.source_passage: Those wasted hours ought to be spent on far more interesting and rewarding subject matters that will build up knowledge and vocabulary, and therefore induce greater reading competence.

claims.text: Current educational standards lack the specificity and insight into the nature of reading required for meaningful accountability.
claims.source_passage: The standards have not told them with adequate specificity or adequate insight into the actual nature of reading.

claims.text: Standards must serve as guides to specific curricular content to function as a foundation for fair and productive tests.
claims.source_passage: Only standards that are guides to curricular content can be foundations for fair and productive tests.

claims.text: Fair and productive accountability in reading is impossible without definite content standards.
claims.source_passage: But they must come to understand that without definite content standards, there can be no fair and productive accountability in reading.

claims.text: Authorities have a responsibility to state exactly what children need to learn and schools need to teach in every grade.
claims.source_passage: The authorities can’t just wave their hands and imperiously demand better reading without stating what children need to learn and schools need to teach in a given grade in order to gradually reach that long-range goal.

claims.text: Punitive threats to teachers attached to current reading tests should be removed as an intermediate policy step.
claims.source_passage: Meanwhile, a positive intermediate step would be to remove the punitive threats to teachers attached to our educationally invalid reading tests—the subject of the next chapter.

claims.text: The removal of high-stakes pressure would allow educators to focus on the long-term acquisition of knowledge.
claims.source_passage: If the high stakes were removed, sensible teachers and principals would be willing to pay more attention to the long arc of knowledge acquisition, which is the route to producing good readers and competent high school graduates.

claims.text: Current reading tests make fraudulent claims when used to measure what schools have actually taught.
claims.source_passage: To sum up: This chapter has shown that recent, technically valid reading tests make fraudulent claims if they are used to gauge what the schools have taught.

claims.text: Existing reading tests are consequentially invalid because they do more harm than good and narrow the focus of schooling.
claims.source_passage: They are “consequentially invalid.” They do more harm than good.

claims.text: Topic familiarity is a stronger predictor of reading comprehension than the formal ability to handle difficult syntax.
claims.source_passage: (This result anticipated recent work showing that topic familiarity, often indicated by word familiarity, is a stronger predictor of comprehension than formal ability to deal with difficult syntax.)

claims.text: Initiation into the public sphere is a fundamental aim of education.
claims.source_passage: Since a valid reading test probes a student’s degree of initiation into the public sphere—a fundamental aim of education—any policy that lowers or neglects to improve test scores in reading is a failed educational policy.

claims.text: Following the education summits of 1989 and 1996, every US state except Iowa established grade-by-grade reading standards.
claims.source_passage: After the governors’ education summits of 1989 and 1996, all but one state (Iowa) developed grade-by-grade reading standards on which schooling in the state would be based.

claims.text: The improvement in teaching decoding led to improved comprehension specifically for simple, undemanding texts in early grades.
claims.source_passage: I mentioned one consequence of these reforms: an improvement in the teaching of decoding... That in turn should also have produced an improvement in reading comprehension, and it did so for undemanding texts on everyday topics in the early grades.

claims.text: Students in large American cities take approximately eight standardized tests per year, which consumes over 4 percent of total school time.
claims.source_passage: an alarming study recently produced by the Council of Great City Schools reporting that each student in the large cities now takes about eight standardized tests in a year. Just sitting for the tests takes over 4 percent of school time.

claims.text: The unpredictability of test topics is an inherent and necessary feature of all-purpose reading tests when the school curriculum is not standardized.
claims.source_passage: That is an expected, even obvious, characteristic of an all-purpose sort of reading test, which is, of course, the only sort that could possibly be fair when the topics of the school curriculum are unknown.

claims.text: Standardized tests such as the Gates-MacGinitie and Iowa tests demonstrate high internal reliability with correlation scores of approximately 0.9.
claims.source_passage: Gates and Iowa each show strong internal correlations between different forms of the same test (about .9 on a scale in which 1 is perfect).

claims.text: There is a scientific consensus among cognitive scientists that relevant knowledge is the primary driver of reading comprehension once decoding is mastered.
claims.source_passage: But once decoding has been mastered and fluency attained, relevant knowledge becomes the chief component of reading skill. Every cognitive scientist specializing in the subject would agree with that statement.

claims.text: Parents should take an active role in demanding that schools replace 'knowledge-displacing' skill exercises with substantive content in history, science, and the arts.
claims.source_passage: A positive aim of parents should be to demand knowledge-building substance in their child’s language arts classroom, to replace exercises that are knowledge-displacing, soul-deadening, and essentially useless after ten lessons.

claims.text: Accountability proponents have a duty to join with local stakeholders to define the specific grade-by-grade knowledge students are expected to learn.
claims.source_passage: Accountability hawks need to show more grit in creating state and local curriculum standards. They should join with parents and teachers to help define the grade-by-grade knowledge that all the children in their local purview should gain.

claims.text: Teachers face professional risks, including job security, if they do not comply with instructions to prioritize test preparation over content instruction.
claims.source_passage: The comments by teachers on my piece were sobering. They carried this message: “Well, Mr. Hirsch, that’s all very well, but if you were in my shoes you’d realize that your job would be at stake if you did not do test preparation as instructed.”

evidence.description: NAEP 2012 long-term trend report showing a statistically significant three-point drop in reading scores for 17-year-olds compared to 1988-1992.
evidence.source_reference: NAEP 2012 Long Term Trend Reading and Mathematics Assessments
evidence.source_passage: Figure 1.1 is taken from the 2012 long-term trend report of the National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP)—the latest nationwide analysis we have for long-term reading trends among seventeen-year-olds. It shows a statistically significant drop of three points in the reading abilities of seventeen-year-olds...

evidence.description: Analysis of the Armed Forces Qualification Test (AFQT) showing that vocabulary size is the strongest predictor of comprehension and overall competence.
evidence.source_reference: Pamela Ing, Carole A. Lunney, and Randall J. Olsen, Reanalysis of the 1980 AFQT Data from the NLSY791 (2007)
evidence.source_passage: Analyses of the Armed Forces Qualification Test (AFQT) showed that the vocabulary size test and the paragraph comprehension test correlate so well that they can be considered to represent the same underlying competence, which was most accurately measured by vocabulary size!

evidence.description: Correlation between basic skills (specifically verbal ability) and the black-white earnings gap.
evidence.source_reference: William R. Johnson and Derek Neal, 'Basic Skills and the Black-White Earnings Gap' (1998)
evidence.source_passage: See also: William R. Johnson and Derek Neal, 'Basic Skills and the Black-White Earnings Gap,' in The Black-White Test Score Gap...

evidence.description: NAEP (National Assessment of Educational Progress) results showing stagnant or declining scores and widening gaps in older students.
A Nation at Risk (1983) report documenting the decline of American education in the 1960s and 70s.
The Council of Great City Schools report showing urban students take about eight standardized tests a year, occupying 4% of school time.
evidence.source_reference: Council of Great City Schools, 2015

evidence.description: US Department of Education report (October 2015) expressing concern over excessive testing.
evidence.source_reference: US Department of Education, 2015

evidence.description: Metastudies on the plateau effect of reading strategy instruction.
evidence.source_reference: Rosenshine and Meister, 'Reciprocal Teaching', 1994; Willingham and Lovette, 2014

evidence.description: NAEP data showing stagnant reading scores for seventeen-year-olds despite decades of strategy-focused instruction.
evidence.source_reference: National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP)
evidence.source_passage: That’s how we know that our nine- and thirteen-year-olds have improved, and our seventeen-year-olds have not.

evidence.description: Internal reliability correlations of approximately .9 for well-calibrated reading tests like Gates and Iowa.
evidence.source_reference: Gates-MacGinitie; Iowa Test of Basic Skills
evidence.source_passage: Gates and Iowa each show strong internal correlations between different forms of the same test (about .9 on a scale in which 1 is perfect).

evidence.description: Study showing that vocabulary size is a stronger predictor of comprehension than formal ability to deal with syntax.
evidence.source_reference: Ing, Lunney, and Olsen, 'Reanalysis of the 1980 AFQT Data', 2007
evidence.source_passage: This result anticipated recent work showing that topic familiarity, often indicated by word familiarity, is a stronger predictor of comprehension than formal ability to deal with difficult syntax.

evidence.description: Lack of improvement in student reading scores despite pervasive schooling in 'main-idea finding' strategies.
Consensus among cognitive scientists that knowledge is the primary component of reading skill post-decoding.
Reports of students in Palm Beach County and elsewhere experiencing physical and mental distress (Xanax use, school refusal) due to testing stress.
evidence.source_reference: Alvarez, Lizette. 'States Listen as Parents Give Rampant Testing an F,' New York Times, Nov 9, 2014

evidence.description: Comparison of Texas Grade 3-5 math standards (which specify equivalent fractions and unequal denominators) vs. Texas Grade 3-8 reading standards (which repeat the abstract task of summarizing main ideas).
A comparison of Grade 3 through Grade 8 reading standards showing identical 'main idea' requirements across years.
A list of Virginia reading standards (Grades 2-8) that all use the exact phrase 'Identify the main idea.'
Analysis of Common Core standards showing minimal incremental change (e.g., from 'one main idea' to 'two or more') across several grade levels.
Released test 'stems' from Texas state exams that phrase questions in terms of 'main idea' and 'inferencing skills.'
evidence.source_reference: Texas tests, released items for 5th and 7th graders

evidence.description: A review of research on strategy instruction showing that it reaches a plateau quickly and is most effective when limited to a short period (approx. 10 lessons).
evidence.source_reference: Willingham and Lovette, 'Can Reading Comprehension Be Taught?' (2014)
evidence.source_passage: The evidence is summarized in a recent review article by Willingham and Lovette, “Can Reading Comprehension Be Taught?” They answer: “Not really.”

evidence.description: Sample items released from Texas state tests showing the focus on 'main idea' and 'inferencing' stems.
evidence.source_reference: Released test questions in Texas for fifth and seventh graders
evidence.source_passage: I offer examples from Texas and Virginia... these stems were taken from released test questions in Texas for fifth and seventh graders.

evidence.description: Research showing that making students self-conscious about strategy use can be nonadaptive and distract from performance.
evidence.source_reference: Robert S. Siegler, 'Adaptive and Nonadaptive Characteristics of Low-Income Children’s Mathematical Strategy Use'
evidence.source_passage: making young students become highly self-conscious about applying strategies distracts their attention and degrades their performance.

evidence.description: The persistent lack of improvement in national reading abilities despite decades of strategy-based accountability policies.
evidence.source_reference: Noted as 'brute fact' by author
evidence.source_passage: A further piece of evidence is the simple brute fact that the policy has not improved the reading abilities of our students when they emerge from our schooling.

evidence.description: Sample Grade 3 PARCC test question asking students to define 'avenge' and provide supporting details.
Sample Grade 4 Smarter Balanced question asking for the meaning of 'disgorging' based on context clues about exploding stars.
Comments from teachers on Hirsch's Huffington Post piece stating they must perform test preparation or risk their jobs.
Reanalysis of 1980 AFQT data showing vocabulary size tests and paragraph comprehension tests correlate so highly they measure the same underlying competence.
evidence.source_reference: Pamela Ing, Carole A. Lunney, and Randall J. Olsen, Reanalysis of the 1980 AFQT Data from the NLSY791 (2007)
evidence.source_passage: Analyses of the Armed Forces Qualification Test (AFQT) showed that the vocabulary size test and the paragraph comprehension test correlate so well that they can be considered to represent the same underlying competence.

evidence.description: Evidence that skill-based policies have failed to improve reading abilities in students graduating from the US school system.
evidence.source_reference: Willingham and Lovette, 'Can Reading Comprehension Be Taught?' (2014)
evidence.source_passage: A further piece of evidence is the simple brute fact that the policy has not improved the reading abilities of our students when they emerge from our schooling.

evidence.description: A study examining the evolution of mental ability and its relationship to economic success and schooling, providing empirical data on the long-term predictive value of verbal and mental skills.
evidence.source_reference: Christopher Winship and Sanders D. Korenman, 'Economic Success and the Evolution of Schooling and Mental Ability' (1999)

evidence.description: An empirical study investigating how syntactic and lexical complexity specifically affects the comprehension of elementary science texts among students.
evidence.source_reference: Diana J. Arya, Elfrieda H. Hiebert, and P. David Pearson, 'The Effects of Syntactic and Lexical Complexity on the Comprehension of Elementary Science Texts' (2011)

evidence.description: A critical consumer guide and meta-analysis of various reading comprehension assessments used for adolescents, evaluating their technical reliability and construct validity.
evidence.source_reference: Leila Morsy, Michael Kieffer, and Catherine Snow, 'Measure for Measure: A Critical Consumers’ Guide to Reading Comprehension Assessments for Adolescents' (2010)

evidence.description: A foundational research report from the Educational Testing Service defining the theoretical framework for the validity of test interpretation and use.
evidence.source_reference: Samuel Messick, 'Validity of Test Interpretation and Use' (1990)

evidence.description: Statistical reporting on the growth of the 'opt-out' movement in New York State as a reaction against Common Core testing.
evidence.source_reference: Elizabeth A. Harris and Ford Fessenden, 'Opt Out Becomes Anti-Test Rallying Cry in New York State,' New York Times, May 20, 2015

concepts.author_definition: The arena of civic and economic life requiring a common language and general knowledge for effective participation.
concepts.common_alternative: Often used more abstractly in political science to refer to locations of debate; Hirsch uses it specifically as the target of educational initiation.
concepts.source_passage: it gauges a student’s ability to operate effectively within the public sphere. Since a valid reading test probes a student’s degree of initiation into the public sphere—a fundamental aim of education...

concepts.author_definition: The ability to sound out words fast and effortlessly (decoding and fluency), as opposed to higher-level comprehension.
concepts.common_alternative: Often simply called 'reading' in early childhood education circles, which Hirsch disputes.
concepts.source_passage: the testing regimens have clearly helped improve the mechanics of early reading (that is, the ability to sound out fast and effortlessly)—an important gain.

concepts.author_definition: A proxy for the total accumulated knowledge and vocabulary a student has acquired since birth, serving as a predictor of life outcomes.
The mechanical process of turning written marks into sounds and words, essentially 'sounding out' text.
A 'plant of slow growth' that reflects the cumulative knowledge acquired through all experiences from birth and is the decisive factor in mature reading comprehension.
Content-independent strategies like 'summarizing', 'questioning the author', or 'finding the main idea' that are incorrectly believed to be the keys to reading comprehension.
The principle that a test's technical validity is secondary to its actual impact on educational outcomes; if a test causes self-defeating educational practices, it is consequentially invalid.
concepts.source_passage: His phrase was “consequential validity.” His key insight was that the technical validity of a test is of little value by itself. The point of educational testing is to help education.

concepts.author_definition: The ability to understand the complex language and specialized vocabulary found in newspapers, textbooks, and political speeches (e.g., Keynes's 'The Economic Consequences of the Peace').
concepts.common_alternative: Basic decoding or simple narrative reading
concepts.source_passage: They can read Stupendous Sports Stadiums, but not The Economic Consequences of the Peace.

concepts.author_definition: Educational standards that specify formal skills (like finding the main idea) without specifying the substantive content or knowledge that students should learn.
A defect in a test that lies not in the test's construction but in the harmful, self-defeating educational practices it encourages in schools.
An alleged 'all-purpose' strategy for identifying the core message of a text, which Hirsch argues is a myth because the ability to find a main idea depends on topic-specific knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: A transferable cognitive skill that can be improved through practice regardless of the text's subject matter.

concepts.author_definition: The failure of standards-makers to specify concrete knowledge or topics in reading curricula due to a fear of the political controversy that follows defining shared knowledge.
The state of American classrooms where instruction and testing are based on the false premise that reading comprehension is a content-independent skill.
A normative standard where a test is only fair if it measures knowledge and vocabulary that the school has explicitly been mandated to teach.
Techniques like inference-making, predicting, and finding the main idea that are treated as transferable skills independent of subject matter.
concepts.common_alternative: Cognitive skills that can be taught and applied to any text to improve comprehension.
concepts.source_passage: The substance of the reading curriculum had been described in the content-evading state standards as consisting of all-purpose techniques like inference making, predicting, and main-idea finding.

concepts.author_definition: A specific strategy emphasized by the Common Core, which the author views as just another 'content-free' strategy masquerading as a general skill.
concepts.common_alternative: A critical analysis of a text that focuses on significant details or patterns.
concepts.source_passage: The new kind of inference is to be connected with an additional inference strategy called “close reading”... These are the Common Core standards that are the most content-free, strategy-like.

concepts.author_definition: Educational standards that avoid specifying grade-by-grade knowledge and instead focus on abstract skills to give the appearance of rigor without requiring a shared curriculum.
concepts.common_alternative: Skills-based standards that allow for local flexibility in content.
concepts.source_passage: The substance of the reading curriculum had been described in the content-evading state standards as consisting of all-purpose techniques

concepts.author_definition: Hirsch critiques the 'deductive-syllogistic model' of inference used in tests, suggesting instead a 'part-whole situation model' where meaning is inferred from the overall context and background knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: The ability to derive a single correct meaning from text using logic alone.
concepts.source_passage: inferences tend to be of the part-whole situation model, not the deductive-syllogistic model.

concepts.author_definition: A test that is technically accurate in its measurements but harmful in its practical effects on educational behavior and curriculum.
concepts.common_alternative: Often validity is only discussed in technical terms of measurement accuracy (construct validity).
concepts.source_passage: They are “consequentially invalid.” They do more harm than good.

concepts.author_definition: The multi-year, cumulative process of building vocabulary and domain knowledge which eventually results in high reading proficiency.
The process by which students gain the general knowledge and vocabulary necessary to operate effectively in society.
The phenomenon where efforts to raise test scores through drills actually cause scores to drop by displacing the knowledge building required for mature reading.
Classroom activities focused on abstract skills (like finding the main idea) that take up time better spent on subject-matter learning.
Policy makers and advocates who strongly emphasize testing and school accountability measures.
A trifling or ineffective result, used to describe the predicted impact of future skill-based Common Core testing.

cases.name: NCLB Cohort (2002-2012)
cases.description: Students who were seven in 2002 and seventeen in 2012. This cohort experienced ten years of high-stakes testing under NCLB. Despite reaching record highs at age thirteen (2008), they showed a statistically significant decline in reading ability by age seventeen.
key_passages: Students who were seven in 2002 would have been old NCLB hands in 2012. They would have experienced almost ten years of high-stakes testing in reading under NCLB.
This 2012 result for seventeen-year-olds seems particularly anomalous, because that same cohort four years earlier, at age thirteen, had attained the topmost score that NAEP had ever recorded
contestable_aspects: Whether the decline was caused by NCLB or other social/cultural factors.

cases.name: No Child Left Behind Act (NCLB)
cases.description: Federal legislation enacted in 2002 that mandated grade-by-grade reading standards and high-stakes testing. Hirsch notes it improved decoding but led to a decline in knowledge-based instruction.
key_passages: The No Child Left Behind Act, which was signed into law in 2002, mandated standards and yearly high-stakes testing in reading and math based on the state standards.
Younger students can now decode texts with more fluency and accuracy than they did before NCLB.

cases.name: Common Core State Standards
cases.description: The current framework for state-level standards and testing, which Hirsch predicts will fail for the same reasons as NCLB if it continues the focus on skills over knowledge.
key_passages: high-stakes reading tests that accompany the new Common Core State Standards are going to have a similar nugatory or depressing effect on the reading competence of our seventeen-year-olds

cases.name: Strategy-centric Primary Education
cases.description: The current mode of schooling where teachers are told that subject matter is secondary and that comprehension strategies can be taught using trivial texts like 'Tyler Makes Pancakes!' rather than substantive content like biographies of Lincoln.
key_passages: Teachers have been told that subject matter is secondary, that they can teach strategies just as well with Tyler Makes Pancakes! or Stupendous Sports Stadiums as with a biography of Abraham Lincoln.

cases.name: The Opt-Out Movement
cases.description: A growing protest by parents and teachers against excessive testing and test preparation. It is characterized by significant numbers of districts refusing tests, teachers complaining about the narrowing of the curriculum, and parents reporting extreme stress and health issues in their children.
key_passages: The opt-out protest against excessive testing and test preparation has spread to significant numbers of districts.
Parents railed at a system that they said was overrun by new tests coming from all levels—district, state and federal.

cases.name: Texas Education Standards
cases.description: Hirsch compares the specific, building-block nature of Texas math standards (fractions) with the vague, repetitive reading standards that focus solely on 'main ideas' from grade 3 through grade 8.
key_passages: Current math standards are much better guides for teachers and test makers than are current standards in reading.
Wary of specifying either topics or texts, the standards makers focus on the skill of finding the main idea.

cases.name: Virginia Reading Standards
cases.description: The author cites the Virginia Standards of Learning as a prime example of 'content emptiness,' where from grade 2 through grade 8, the curriculum requirement for reading is simply the repetitive instruction to 'Identify the main idea.'
key_passages: My own state of Virginia is more forthright about the inherent repetitiousness and content emptiness of its reading standards: Grade 2: 'g) Identify the main idea.' Grade 3: 'g) Identify the main idea.' ... Grade 8: 'h) Identify the main idea.'

cases.name: Texas Standardized Tests (STAAR)
cases.description: The author uses released test questions from Texas to demonstrate how test items are framed to look like they are testing strategies (like inferencing or summarizing) while they are actually testing the student's ability to understand the specific content of the passage.
key_passages: Here are some 'stems' taken from released items on the Texas tests. Their form indicates misleadingly that strategy expertise rather than specific knowledge is being probed.
To keep Texas still in view, these stems were taken from released test questions in Texas for fifth and seventh graders.

cases.name: Texas and Virginia State Testing
cases.description: The author examines released items from Texas and Virginia because they did not initially adopt Common Core, providing examples of 'traditional' strategy-based testing. These tests use question stems that pretend to measure 'inferencing skills' but actually rely on passage familiarity.
key_passages: I offer examples from Texas and Virginia, since those states did not sign on to the Common Core, and the standards are in force.
Their form indicates misleadingly that strategy expertise rather than specific knowledge is being probed.

cases.name: Common Core Reading Tests
cases.description: The new generation of tests intended to replace state-specific tests. Hirsch argues that despite their novelty and expense, they replicate the old error of testing content-free 'skills' like close reading because they lack a specific knowledge-based curriculum to test against.
key_passages: the makers of the Common Core tests are placed in the same position as the makers of the previous tests that were based on state standards.
The sample items made public by the new test consortia for the Common Core amply fulfill my prediction that the tests will stress both main-idea finding and close reading.

cases.name: PARCC and Smarter Balanced Consortia
cases.description: The two primary multi-state testing consortia developed to create assessments for the Common Core State Standards. Hirsch uses their sample items to show that the new tests are identical in flaw to the old ones.
key_passages: The sample items made public by the new test consortia for the Common Core amply fulfill my prediction that the tests will stress both main-idea finding and close reading.
Thus PARCC, one of the two test consortia, has developed two-part questions...
The 'Smarter Balanced' consortium puts the two-part structure into a single question...
contestable_aspects: The intent of the test developers regarding 'close reading' vs. 'background knowledge'.

thinkers.name: Pamela Ing, Carole A. Lunney, and Randall J. Olsen
thinkers.key_idea: Paragraph comprehension and vocabulary size measure the same underlying factor, making vocabulary the more efficient measure.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses their analysis of AFQT data to justify his focus on vocabulary as the primary indicator of reading comprehension and general knowledge.
key_passages: The apparent fact that the two tests [vocabulary and comprehension] measure the same underlying factor suggests an efficient alternative for computing an AFQT is to simply drop the difficult-to-administer paragraph comprehension items and focus on the word-knowledge test.

thinkers.name: Arne Duncan and Barack Obama
thinkers.key_idea: Excessive and poor quality testing takes valuable time away from classroom learning and creativity.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses their October 2015 'Testing Action Plan' as evidence that even the political establishment has begun to recognize the negative consequences of over-testing.
key_passages: the too-frequent testing of students in American schools has at last become a subject of concern and even self-criticism by the US Secretary of Education and the President

thinkers.name: Barak Rosenshine and Carla Meister
thinkers.key_idea: Formal reading strategies have a quick plateau in effectiveness.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites their review of reciprocal teaching to support his claim that drills in formal skills do not lead to continuous improvement in reading scores.
key_passages: their progress quickly reaches a limit and then halts. We know this from various metastudies as well as from the stagnant NAEP data.

thinkers.name: Samuel Messick
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Messick's theory to argue that even 'good' tests (technically valid) are 'bad' (educationally invalid) if they lead to poor teaching practices.
key_passages: That is to say, they are technically valid but educationally invalid, a distinction brought into prominence by the important testing theorist Samuel Messick. His phrase was “consequential validity.”

thinkers.name: Daniel T. Willingham & Gail Lovette
thinkers.key_idea: Reading comprehension strategies are quickly learned and offer limited utility beyond a short initial instructional period.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses their review article as the primary scientific evidence to debunk the practice of long-term strategy drills in schools.
key_passages: The evidence is summarized in a recent review article by Willingham and Lovette, “Can Reading Comprehension Be Taught?” They answer: “Not really.”

thinkers.name: Robert S. Siegler
thinkers.key_idea: Self-conscious strategy application can be nonadaptive and hinder performance for certain students.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Siegler to support the claim that the current focus on metacognition can actually be counterproductive for students.
key_passages: Worse, making young students become highly self-conscious about applying strategies distracts their attention and degrades their performance.

actors.name: NAEP (National Assessment of Educational Progress)
actors.context: The source of long-term trend data for reading and math scores used to analyze the impact of educational reforms.

actors.name: Anti-testing activists
actors.context: Parents and students boycotting tests; Hirsch argues their activism is 'misguided' and that the problem is the quality/basis of the tests, not testing itself.

actors.name: Council of Great City Schools
actors.context: Published a study on the volume of standardized testing in urban school districts.

actors.name: US Department of Education
actors.context: Issued a report/fact sheet in 2015 critiquing excessive testing.

actors.name: Superintendents and Principals
actors.context: Instituting test-prep programs based on the theory that strategies are the key to reading.

actors.name: No Child Left Behind (NCLB) authors
actors.context: Hirsch refers to the 'credit' of NCLB while preparing to critique the resulting testing regime.

actors.name: No Child Left Behind Act
actors.context: Cited for its successful focus on improving the mechanics of decoding in early reading.

actors.name: Texas Education Agency
actors.context: Used as an example of contrasting standards between math (specific) and reading (vague).

actors.name: Palm Beach County Schools
actors.context: Locale mentioned in a New York Times report regarding parent protests and student test-related stress.

actors.name: Makers of standards and tests
actors.context: Accused of creating artificial, politically expedient constructs of reading comprehension.

actors.name: Common Core State Standards
actors.context: Identified as being part of the 'lack of real progression or content' despite their newness.

actors.name: Texas Education Agency
actors.context: Provider of the released test items used as examples of invalid testing.

actors.name: Common Core Test Consortia
actors.context: The groups designing new tests that Hirsch argues are repeating the mistakes of the past.

actors.name: Teachers
actors.context: Responding to Hirsch, explaining their job-related pressure to do test prep.

actors.name: Accountability Hawks
actors.context: Policy makers who demand higher test scores and teacher accountability without providing the specific curriculum standards necessary to achieve them fairly.

actors.name: Parents
actors.context: Hirsch encourages parents to demand knowledge-building substance in classrooms rather than soul-deadening exercises.

objections_raised.objection: Testing is inherently harmful or unnecessary and should be boycotted.
objections_raised.response: Tests are necessary; the solution is to make them fewer and better by basing them on knowledge-based curriculums.

objections_raised.objection: The decline in reading scores between ages thirteen and seventeen is the fault of high schools.
objections_raised.source: Unattributed (described as a likely first inclination)
objections_raised.response: Reading comprehension (vocabulary size) is a 'plant of slow growth' determined by knowledge accumulated since birth; high schools cannot easily undo or repair the lack of early knowledge acquisition.

objections_raised.objection: High schools are to blame for the widening achievement gap and the failure of older students to maintain early reading gains.
objections_raised.response: Vocabulary and comprehension are the result of cumulative knowledge growth starting from birth; the deficit is created by early grade schooling focusing on skills rather than knowledge.

objections_raised.objection: Reading tests probe general skills like main-idea finding and inferencing.
objections_raised.response: They do no such thing; they are actually probing specific knowledge and vocabulary. If they were general skills, the current generation would be scoring better since they are heavily drilled in them.

objections_raised.objection: Test makers argue they cannot create tests that encourage knowledge if the state standards they must follow are content-free.
objections_raised.response: Hirsch agrees that the fault lies with the standards makers who committed 'content cop-outs' to avoid political controversy.

objections_raised.objection: Reading comprehension tests are fair because they probe universal strategies that can be practiced.
objections_raised.response: The author argues this is a 'method' devised to make 'inherently unfair tests fair' by creating an appearance of strategy probing, when success actually depends on passage-specific knowledge.

objections_raised.objection: But aren't there in fact such all-purpose strategies? And won't learning them improve reading?
objections_raised.response: Research shows they offer a small initial boost but plateau after about 10 lessons. Spending more time than that is a waste and can distract students from the actual content.

objections_raised.objection: Teachers cannot focus on content-rich subjects (History, Science) because their job performance is measured by test-preparation success.
objections_raised.response: Hirsch acknowledges this pressure is real but argues that parents and teachers must therefore act to change the character of the tests and demand knowledge-building substance in the classroom.

objections_raised.objection: Accountability and testing are necessary to ensure schools are performing.
objections_raised.response: Hirsch agrees that accountability is needed, but argues it is impossible to have fair or productive accountability without grade-by-grade content standards that define what schools must teach.

cross_chapter_refs.reference_text: This point is amplified with references to research in chapter 6.
The defects of the close-reading standard are discussed in chapter 6, on the Common Core Standards.
This point is amplified with references to research in chapter 6.
The defects of the close-reading standard are discussed in chapter 6, on the Common Core Standards.
This point is amplified with references to research in chapter 6.
The defects of the close-reading standard are discussed in chapter 6, on the Common Core Standards.
a subject I take up in more detail in the next chapter
The defects of the close-reading standard are discussed in chapter 6, on the Common Core Standards.
This point is amplified with references to research in chapter 6.
The defects of the close-reading standard are discussed in chapter 6, on the Common Core Standards.
The defects of the close-reading standard are discussed in chapter 6, on the Common Core Standards.
the subject of the next chapter [punitive threats to teachers]
This point is amplified with references to research in chapter 6.
The defects of the close-reading standard are discussed in chapter 6, on the Common Core Standards.

dependencies.explanation: If cognitive science confirms knowledge is the primary driver of comprehension, then a reading test necessarily functions as a measure of a student's knowledge base.

The claim that tests must be based on specific curriculums to be fair relies on the premise that reading tests are actually tests of knowledge, which can only be fairly tested if the knowledge domain is known.
If unpredictable topics are inherent to non-standardized testing and knowledge is required for comprehension, then unfairness is inevitable without a defined curriculum.
If current policies are failing because they neglect the 'initiation into the public sphere,' parents are justified in demanding a return to substantive content.
Because teachers face professional risks if they deviate from test-prep, the impetus for change must come from outside the school hierarchy (parents).
Evidence that intensive test prep fails to help long-term reading ability justifies the label of 'failed policy' for current accountability regimens.
The claim that schooling has depressed vocabulary growth by age seventeen relies on the premise that vocabulary is a slow-growing cumulative result of knowledge acquisition over time.
The author contrasts the success of NCLB in early fluency with its failure in long-term vocabulary to show that early gains were superficial mechanics.
The normative claim that schools spend too much time on test prep is supported by the empirical claim that high-stakes testing led to the abandonment of history and arts.
The failure of drills to raise mature scores is based on the theoretical observation that these skills reach a ceiling or 'plateau' quickly.
The conclusion that misguided efforts caused low scores depends on the empirical finding that these efforts (drills) failed to produce results in older students.
The prediction regarding Common Core assumes that it replicates the same misguided skills-based instruction and testing logic as NCLB.

If the lack of subject knowledge in early grades results in poor performance in mature discourse, it follows that reading comprehension depends more on that knowledge than on the formal techniques being taught instead.
The conclusion that knowledge is the primary driver of comprehension requires the premise that strategies are minimal and easily mastered, otherwise they would share primary importance.
If generalized skills are a 'myth,' then by process of elimination, the determining factor in comprehension must be the content-specific knowledge used during decoding.
The consequential invalidity of tests is rooted in the fact that they operate under standards that mislead schools about the nature of reading.
The unpredictability of test topics is the mechanism that forces schools into the 'self-defeating' practice of strategy-drilling, which constitutes the consequential invalidity.
If specific question types (inference/main idea) are empirically shown to probe vocabulary/knowledge, then the test as a whole serves as a probe for those elements.
The claim that knowledge becomes the primary component after decoding depends on the success of decoding-based instruction (like NCLB) in isolating the 'mechanics' of reading.
If identifying a main idea is specific to the passage and not transferable, then reading comprehension cannot be an all-purpose skill.
Domain-specific knowledge enabling comprehension directly supports the claim that comprehension is dependent on such knowledge.
The claim that tests are unfair (C62) relies on the premise that tests require specific knowledge that schools haven't taught (C61).
If tests reflect outside knowledge rather than school knowledge, they are an unfair measure of school effectiveness.
Political expediency (C59) is the motive for creating the 'fictitious world' (C70) where metaskills mask the lack of curriculum.
The definition of reading as 'strategies' is a primary component of the fictitious alternative world described in C70.
The Texas test items serve as specific examples of the misleading phrasing used to suggest strategy expertise.
The principle of domain specificity from cognitive science provides the theoretical basis for the claim that general reading skills do not exist.
If general skills like close reading and main-idea finding do not exist, then tests designed to measure them are necessarily based on a false theory.
The fact that reading inferences are 'part-whole situation models' rather than 'deductive-syllogistic' models supports the claim that current tests use the wrong theory of inference.
The predicted failure of new reading tests is based on the conclusion that they continue to follow an invalid theory of comprehension.
C79 applies the general prediction about test failure specifically to the Common Core framework.
Because specific knowledge is required to answer comprehension questions, acquiring that knowledge is the only effective long-range strategy.
The normative claim that knowledge-based schooling closes the gap depends on the theoretical claim that knowledge gains are the primary driver of comprehension.
The failure of skill-based tests to provide productive outcomes necessitates a shift to content-based standards for true accountability.
The proposal to restructure reading like math is the practical implementation of content-specific accountability.
If topic familiarity is the primary driver of comprehension, then standards must specify that topic content to create a fair basis for testing.
The impossibility of fair accountability without content standards directly necessitates that standards serve as content guides.
Because tests use random topics rather than taught content, they cannot validly measure the effectiveness of school instruction.
The fraudulent nature of the measurement (claiming to measure school quality while actually measuring prior knowledge) is a primary reason the tests are considered consequentially invalid.
The displacement of knowledge-building for skill-drills, caused by current testing, is the 'harm' that makes the tests invalid.
The focus on long-term knowledge acquisition is contingent upon removing the short-term punitive pressures that currently mandate test-prep.

warrants.warrant_text: If a specific metric (vocabulary/verbal score) is the most reliable predictor of real-world success, then that metric serves as a valid proxy for the successful 'initiation' of a student into society.
warrants.vulnerability: Correlation does not imply that the test itself is the tool of initiation, only that it tracks it; focusing on the test score might not provide the initiation.

warrants.warrant_text: Scientific consensus regarding cognitive architecture (how the mind processes language) should dictate the structural design of educational assessments.
warrants.vulnerability: Cognitive science describes how the brain works but does not prescribe specific curriculum policies, which involve political and values-based choices.

warrants.warrant_text: Long-term trend data from a consistent national assessment (NAEP) is a reliable indicator of systemic educational efficacy over time.
warrants.vulnerability: Demographic shifts in the student population over 30 years can influence average scores regardless of the quality of instruction.

warrants.warrant_text: If reading standards repetitively emphasize the same abstract skills while math standards build complex content, and reading scores decline while math scores remain stable, then the lack of content-progression in reading is the likely cause of the decline.
warrants.vulnerability: Assumes math and reading are cognitively analogous and that the stability of math scores proves the efficacy of its content-based standards.

warrants.warrant_text: If metastudies show that skill-instruction effects plateau quickly, then continuing to drill those skills past the plateau provides no marginal benefit and constitutes a failure to improve mature competence.
warrants.vulnerability: The plateau might be due to poor implementation of drills rather than the inherent nature of skill instruction.

warrants.warrant_text: Instructional time is a zero-sum resource; time mandated for standardized testing and its preparation is time subtracted from content-rich subjects like history and science.
warrants.vulnerability: Some educators argue that content can be successfully integrated into test-prep exercises.

warrants.warrant_text: If intensive drilling in a specific variable (formal strategies) fails to improve outcomes, while the absence of another variable (knowledge) correlates with failure, the latter is the true causal factor for success.
warrants.vulnerability: Stagnant scores could be caused by poor teaching of strategies rather than the strategies being inherently less important than knowledge.

warrants.warrant_text: High correlation between different reading tests suggests they are all measuring the same stable underlying trait, and since general strategies are a 'myth,' that trait must be the student's accumulated knowledge and vocabulary.
warrants.vulnerability: The stable trait could be general intelligence (IQ) or cognitive processing speed rather than specifically acquired domain knowledge.

warrants.warrant_text: The value of a tool is determined by its systemic outcome; if a scientifically accurate tool (technically valid) produces harmful behavior in the system it measures, it is fundamentally flawed (consequentially invalid).
warrants.vulnerability: This places the blame on the tool for the user's reaction; schools could theoretically maintain a knowledge-rich curriculum despite the test structure.

warrants.warrant_text: If performance on a 'skill' task is binary based on content understanding rather than task repetition, the 'skill' is actually an expression of content knowledge.
warrants.vulnerability: Critics might argue that strategies provide the 'bridge' for students to acquire knowledge from a text even if they start with partial knowledge.

warrants.warrant_text: The existence of clear content standards in one subject (math) proves that content-specificity is technically possible; therefore, its absence in another subject (reading) must be a deliberate choice.
warrants.vulnerability: Math and reading may be inherently different; math is hierarchical/linear while reading content is perceived as more subjective or vast, making the 'choice' of omission logical rather than just political.

warrants.warrant_text: An educational assessment's fairness is defined by its alignment with the specific curriculum provided by the institution being measured.
warrants.vulnerability: Standardized tests often claim to measure 'readiness' or 'aptitude' rather than school achievement, which would make outside knowledge a valid variable to capture.
